llm.label: negative_liberty
llm.rationale: Civil liberty is being maintained (protected from interference), referring to the freedom from arbitrary state power and oppression that unites Dissenters, churchmen, and Catholics.

sentence: If the House looked at the state of the whole of Europe, it would see the necessity of establishing the principles of liberty more firmly in this country.
speaker: Mr. O'Connell

llm.label: negative_liberty
llm.rationale: Establishing the principles of liberty more firmly in this country — liberty here refers to freedom from despotic or oppressive government rule, a core negative liberty value of resisting tyranny.

sentence: The vile Don Miguel persevered in treading down all constitutional freedom.
speaker: Mr. O'Connell

llm.label: negative_liberty
llm.rationale: Constitutional freedom is being suppressed (treaded down) — the speaker invokes freedom from tyrannical interference as a value opposed to the despot's oppression.

sentence: This was a time, therefore, at which the friends of freedom in this country ought to rally.
speaker: Mr. O'Connell

llm.label: negative_liberty
llm.rationale: What is being freed: the nation/country from oppression or external constraint. Friends of freedom are called to rally—a rhetorical invocation of liberty as a political cause opposed to tyranny and despotism.

sentence: This was a time at which the principles of genuine English liberty, as exemplified in the English House of Commons, ought to be asserted and confirmed.
speaker: Mr. O'Connell

llm.label: negative_liberty
llm.rationale: The speaker invokes English liberty as a principle to be asserted and confirmed against implied threats, framing liberty as the core value of parliamentary representation and self-governance—a rhetorical claim about freedom from external constraint or domination.

sentence:
In the poli-
 1216 
tical storms which history described, where was the standard of liberty ever found to be securely planted but on the English soil?
speaker: Mr. O'Connell

llm.label: negative_liberty
llm.rationale: Liberty as a rhetorical invocation of freedom from tyranny and oppression, associated with English soil as a place where it has been protected from political upheaval.

sentence: " If, then, we perceived that an early spirit of liberty existed in this country, and if it were admitted that this spirit had continued to influence the public mind in proportion to the progress of knowledge and general improvement;
speaker: Mr. Robert Dundas

llm.label: negative_liberty
llm.rationale: A "spirit of liberty" existing in and influencing the public mind refers to an ethos or sentiment of non-interference and resistance to tyranny—freedom from oppressive constraints on persons and peoples.

sentence: In former times, owing to the unwillingness of individuals to assert in Parliament the rights of the people, a defect which had been since removed, and owing to the persecution to which the opponents of arbitrary power were then exposed—liberty of speech, and liberty of conscience were suppressed;
speaker: Mr. Robert Dundas

llm.label: negative_liberty
llm.rationale: Freedom of speech and freedom of conscience are named as having been suppressed—these are freedoms from interference with expression and conscience, hence negative liberty.

sentence: But even that inconsistency was done away by my noble friend having given me leave to move for a committee, in which I should have been at liberty to support such a plan of reform, as I might deem not to be injurious.
speaker: Lord Althorp

llm.label: other
llm.rationale: The phrase "at liberty to support such a plan of reform" is a parliamentary procedural expression meaning permitted/allowed to do so; it does not invoke liberty-as-a-value but rather as permission within parliamentary procedure.

sentence: He wished it to be known, that the men who were opposed to what were called popular measures, were, at the same time, attached to rational liberty, and they would resist any inroads that might be made upon it.
speaker: Lord Viscount Valletort

llm.label: negative_liberty
llm.rationale: The speaker asserts attachment to "rational liberty" and resistance to any "inroads" (interferences) upon it—a rhetorical invocation of liberty as a cause opposed to encroachment; the object of freedom is not specified, but the framing is freedom FROM interference/inroads.

sentence: " He adds, indeed, that some precautions are to be regarded, "lest this liberty should go too far," and points out the very provisions which our own Poor-laws prescribe.
speaker: Mr. Sadler

llm.label: negative_liberty
llm.rationale: The "liberty" being discussed is the freedom of the poor or recipients of relief from restrictions—freedom from constraint in their entitlements under Poor-laws. The sentence concerns what safeguards should limit that freedom from interference.

sentence: " Such is the deliberate doctrine of this great authority, the literary father of the English liberty, who to an understanding unmatched in its capacity thus united a benevolence as warm and unbounded.
speaker: Mr. Sadler

llm.label: negative_liberty
llm.rationale: The speaker invokes "English liberty" as a rhetorical ideal associated with a great authority (likely John Locke), presented as a cause opposed to tyranny. This is a rhetorical invocation of liberty as freedom from oppressive constraint.

sentence: in giving employment to that idleness which is now sustained, and consumes, like a canker, the national resources—employment which would be rendered indispensable to any system of relief you would contemplate to introduce, and for which there is in Ireland a present sphere beyond any other country upon earth, you would develope the resources of the country, add to its free liberty, and multiply its wealth;
speaker: Mr. Sadler

llm.label: positive_liberty
llm.rationale: The phrase "add to its free liberty" invokes liberty as a value opposed to the economic dependency and idleness the speaker criticizes. The liberty being discussed is Ireland's capacity to act as an economic and social entity—to develop its resources, to employ its people, to govern its own affairs. This is freedom TO act and TO prosper, not freedom FROM specific interference.

sentence: I will tell those Gentlemen what is the question, The question is the comfort, the support, nay, the very existence of the poor and destitute in Ireland, and if honourable Members can find avocations more worthy of legislators than the serious discussion of this question, they are at liberty to pursue them elsewhere.
speaker: Mr. Sadler

llm.label: negative_liberty
llm.rationale: Members are at liberty to pursue other avocations elsewhere—freedom from constraint on where and how they direct their parliamentary efforts, a negative liberty of non-interference.

sentence: Sir, a short time has elapsed since this House, which had long before swept from these shores the very sight and contamination of slavery, recognized, by a great and godlike act, unexampled by any previous legislative assembly in any age of the world, and in spite of national interests and remonstrances, the liberty of one quarter of the world, and became the armed champion of the un-happiest of mankind.
speaker: Mr. Sadler

llm.label: negative_liberty
llm.rationale: The liberty being recognized is freedom from slavery for people in British colonies and territories ("one quarter of the world"); this is freedom FROM bondage/enslavement, a classic negative liberty.

sentence: said, the object was only to give the greatest possible freedom to the sale of Beer that was consistent with good order.
speaker: The Chancellor of the Exchequer

llm.label: negative_liberty
llm.rationale: Freedom to sell beer (a commercial activity) is being freed from regulatory constraints, consistent with maintaining order—this is negative liberty (freedom from interference with commercial activity).

llm.rationale: Trade with China is being freed from monopolistic restrictions—this is freedom from interference with commercial activity.

sentence: and learned Gentleman sat upon the Opposition side of the House, he was accustomed to admire him as a friend to freedom and an enemy of oppression.
speaker: Mr. Hume

llm.label: negative_liberty
